- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
================================================================================
ISSUE 145 - Unquoted $IGNORES expansion word-splits and glob-expands allowlist lines
SEV: LOW | FILE: .github/workflows/ci.yml
--- DESC ---
`IGNORES` is accumulated as a single string and passed unquoted: `pip-audit ... $IGNORES`. Real advisory IDs (GHSA-*, PYSEC-*, CVE-*) are word-split-safe, so today it works for valid input — but the ci.yml comment invites "one advisory id per line" without forbidding comments, and any line containing spaces (e.g. an accidental `# comment`) is split into multiple bogus `--ignore-vuln` tokens. I reproduced: a `# a comment line` line becomes `--ignore-vuln # a comment line` (4 tokens). pip-audit happens to accept unknown ignore IDs without erroring (I confirmed `--ignore-vuln '# a comment line'` exits 0), so it fails open silently rather than loudly. Combined with the unquoted expansion, a stray glob char in the file could also undergo pathname expansion. This is fragile, fail-open input handling in the security gate.
--- FIX ---
Build an array instead of a string: `args=(); while ...; do args+=(--ignore-vuln "$id"); done` then `pip-audit "${args[@]}"`. Strip comments/whitespace and validate each ID against an expected pattern (e.g. `^(GHSA|PYSEC|CVE)-`).
================================================================================
ISSUE 146 - Over-wide version range for arize-phoenix (>=7.0,<18) admits 10 major versions
SEV: LOW | FILE: pyproject.toml
--- DESC ---
`arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` spans eleven major versions (7 through 17). The installed/resolved version is 17.6.0, ten majors above the floor. A range this wide defeats the point of the cap: any breaking change or a freshly-disclosed advisory in any major 7-17 is admitted by a clean install, and the engine has no lockfile to constrain it (see lockfile finding). Other extras are reasonably bounded (single-major caps), making this one an outlier. Same family is also slightly inconsistent: langgraph>=1.2,<2 is duplicated across the `persistence` and `orchestration` extras (functionally fine, but two sources of truth for one bound).
--- FIX ---
Tighten the phoenix cap to the majors actually tested (e.g. `>=17,<18`, matching the resolved 17.6.0) or whatever range integration CI truly exercises, and de-duplicate the langgraph bound (define it once and reference it) to avoid drift.
================================================================================
ISSUE 147 - No SBOM emission or automated dependency/license/transitive-monitoring in CI
SEV: LOW | FILE: .github/workflows/ci.yml
--- DESC ---
For a production security/privacy engine, the supply-chain story is point-in-time advisory scanning only. There is no SBOM artifact (pip-audit supports `-f cyclonedx-json`/`cyclonedx-xml` and cyclonedx-python-lib is already pulled in transitively), no `.github/dependabot.yml` (no automated transitive-update/PR monitoring), and no automated license check. I scanned all 230 installed dists for strong copyleft: the only two 'gpl' substring hits were false positives (matplotlib = PSF, pandas = BSD-3-Clause), so the license posture is currently clean — but nothing in CI would catch a future transitive dep introducing a GPL/AGPL component into this Apache-2.0 project, or a newly-disclosed CVE between PRs.
--- FIX ---
Emit and archive an SBOM in the audit job (`pip-audit -f cyclonedx-json -o sbom.json` as a CI artifact), add a `.github/dependabot.yml` (pip ecosystem) for transitive-update PRs, and add a lightweight license allowlist check (e.g. pip-licenses or cyclonedx license gate) so copyleft creep fails CI.

All verifications complete. Here is my assessment.

**Summary of findings:**

- **#130** (pip-audit --strict): CONFIRMED. ci.yml:110 now runs `pip-audit --strict --require-hashes`. Test reads real ci.yml via `_ci_text()`; reverting to bare invocation fails the test.
- **#131** (newline-agnostic allowlist parse): CONCERN. The ci.yml:105 loop is correctly hardened (`while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]` + comment skip). But the behavioral regression test exercises a *hardcoded copy* (`_PARSE_LOOP`) in the test file, NOT the real ci.yml loop. Reverting the actual ci.yml loop to the buggy form leaves all 7 tests green.
- **#132** (lockfile/reproducibility): CONFIRMED. Real `requirements.lock` (218 exact pins, sha256 hashes), audit scans `-r requirements.lock --require-hashes`, plus a `uv pip compile` staleness guard. Multiple `_ci_text()`/lock-content tests discriminate.
- **#145** (glob/word-split): CONCERN. ci.yml uses the glob-safe array form `"${IGNORES[@]}"`. The fix is correct, but same gap as #131 — the glob test runs the test-file copy, not the real artifact.
- **#146** (over-wide phoenix range + langgraph dup): CONCERN. The *substantive* risk (fresh install drifting to a vulnerable phoenix) is neutralized by the pinned/hashed lockfile (`arize-phoenix==17.6.0`). But neither of the fix's literal suggestions was applied: pyproject.toml:55 still has `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18`, and langgraph is still duplicated (lines 60, 66). No test guards the pyproject range or dedup.
- **#147** (SBOM): CONFIRMED. ci.yml:111-124 emits a CycloneDX SBOM and uploads it. Test reads real ci.yml; reverting fails it.

Relevant files: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/requirements.lock`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_supply_chain.py`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml`.

Note: an unrelated, pre-existing modification to `src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py` (a `contextlib.nullcontext()` that disables per-conn_str locking, tied to #129) exists in this worktree; it is not part of my batch and does not affect these supply-chain verifications.

verdicts.notes: Read ci.yml:96-110 — audit job now runs `pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"`, directly resolving the no-strict silent-skip defect. Test test_pip_audit_runs_strict_and_require_hashes (test_supply_chain.py:52-58) reads the REAL ci.yml via _ci_text() and asserts the literal 'pip-audit --strict --require-hashes'. Discrimination: edited ci.yml:110 to drop '--strict --require-hashes' -> test FAILED with AssertionError 'audit gate must run pip-audit with --strict --require-hashes (#130)'; restored -> PASS. Scope fine; --require-hashes ties scan to the hashed lockfile, no engine/dataset impact.

Fix is genuinely present: ci.yml:103-108 uses `while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]` with `case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue` — newline-agnostic and comment/blank tolerant, fixing the dropped-final-line bug. BUT the regression test does NOT discriminate against a revert of the real artifact. test_allowlist_parse_is_newline_agnostic_and_comment_tolerant (test_supply_chain.py:97-122) runs a HARDCODED copy (_PARSE_LOOP, lines 26-36) of the loop, not the loop extracted from ci.yml. I reverted the ACTUAL ci.yml loop (lines 103-110) to the buggy `IGNORES=""; while read -r id; do ... done` unquoted-string form and ran the full file: all 7 tests still PASSED (false green). The test only discriminates if you also revert the in-test copy: mutating _PARSE_LOOP to the buggy form makes it FAIL ('final newline-less id dropped (#131)') and also leaks the comment ('--ignore-vuln # a comment'). So the behavioral logic is correctly guarded, but the binding to the real CI artifact is not — a maintainer reverting ci.yml's loop would ship the bug with green CI. No config-level guard (no _ci_text() assertion of the loop) exists. No regression/scope issue otherwise.

requirements.lock is committed (4692 lines, 218 exact == pins, sha256 hashes; arize-phoenix==17.6.0 etc.), generated by `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes`. Audit job scans it with `-r requirements.lock --require-hashes` (ci.yml:110) and a staleness guard regenerates+diffs it (ci.yml:89-95). Three tests cover it. Discrimination verified: (a) removing '-r requirements.lock' and 'uv pip compile'/'requirements.lock is stale' from ci.yml -> test_audit_scans_the_committed_lockfile_not_the_live_env AND test_lockfile_sync_is_enforced_in_ci both FAILED; (b) replacing '--hash=sha256:' with another token in the lock -> test_lockfile_is_fully_pinned_and_hashed FAILED on the hash assertion. Restored, all clean. Reproducibility half of pass-1 #8 genuinely closed; no ADR-0002/0003 impact.

Fix present and correct: ci.yml builds an array IGNORES=() and passes `"${IGNORES[@]}"` (ci.yml:103-110), which is word-split- and glob-safe — fixing the unquoted $IGNORES expansion. Verified the underlying defect/fix manually: the buggy unquoted-string form expands `GHSA-*` to a real file `GHSA-real-file` in cwd, while the array form keeps it literal. test_allowlist_ids_do_not_glob_expand (test_supply_chain.py:125-139) asserts the safe behavior. BUT, like #131, it runs the HARDCODED _PARSE_LOOP copy, not ci.yml. Reverting the REAL ci.yml loop to the buggy unquoted-string form left all 7 tests green. The test DOES discriminate against the in-test copy: I mutated _PARSE_LOOP to a `set -- $IGNORES` unquoted-expansion form and the test FAILED ('glob metacharacter expanded against cwd (#145)', output showed GHSA-real-file). So the logic is guarded but not bound to the CI artifact that actually runs. No scope/regression issue.

The SUBSTANTIVE risk (a fresh install resolving any phoenix 7-17, possibly vulnerable, with no pinning backstop) is genuinely neutralized: requirements.lock pins arize-phoenix==17.6.0 with hashes and the audit scans it with --require-hashes, so installs/audits are now reproducible. This is a legitimate alternative resolution. HOWEVER the fix's two literal recommendations were NOT applied: pyproject.toml:55 still declares `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` (11 majors, unchanged) and langgraph is still duplicated (`langgraph>=1.2,<2` at pyproject.toml:60 and :66, not de-duplicated). The cited test test_lockfile_is_fully_pinned_and_hashed only guards that the LOCK has no ranges — its per-line check `'>=' not in ln and '<' not in ln.split(';')[0]` runs ONLY over lines containing '==', so it inspects nothing in pyproject and nothing about phoenix specifically. Discrimination check: I replaced a lock line `aiohttp==3.14.1` with `aiohttp>=3.14,<4` (no '=='); the test still PASSED because the ranged line is excluded from the 'pinned' set — so the test does not even reliably catch a range injected into the lock. Net: risk addressed via lockfile, but the named pyproject fixes are absent and the test does not discriminate on the phoenix range/dedup. Low severity, no ADR impact.

ci.yml:111-124 adds an SBOM step: `pip-audit ... -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json` plus an actions/upload-artifact@v4 step (name: sbom-cyclonedx). Resolves the 'no SBOM artifact' half of the finding. test_sbom_is_emitted (test_supply_chain.py:91-94) reads REAL ci.yml and asserts 'cyclonedx-json' and 'sbom'. Discrimination: mutated ci.yml replacing 'cyclonedx-json'->other token and 'sbom.cyclonedx.json'->'artifact.json' etc. -> test FAILED; restored -> PASS. Note: the finding also suggested dependabot.yml and a license check; those are NOT implemented (no .github/dependabot.yml), but the SBOM emission — the explicitly-cited test target and primary deliverable — is genuinely present and guarded. Scope appropriate for a LOW finding; no engine impact.
